petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented company director and media specialist seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in business. She holds a Bachelor of Science in IT, an MBA, and a doctorate based on published papers regarding the correlation between certain subjects. She claims to be the director of 8 companies in the media field (5 based in the USA) and works as a professional speaker, author, and television host, with companies established across 4 continents including the USA, Australia, Singapore, and Dubai.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a March 10, 2012 Award from a chapter association that is part of a larger organization; (2) evidence of being among 'more than 3300 people' nominated for an award in 2009; (3) three certificates showing she was a 'Finalist' for an award in 2007 in the categories of 'Self-Help: Inspirational,' 'Health: Psychology,' and 'Self-Help: Relationships'; (4) a certificate stating she was a 'Finalist' in 2008 in the 'Parenting & Family' category; and (5) a certificate stating she was a 'Winner' in 2007 for a category.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the chapter award reflected only local recognition rather than a nationally or internationally recognized prize. For the nomination, the AAO held that earning a nomination is not equivalent to receiving a recognized prize or award. For the finalist certificates, the AAO held that selection as a finalist is not a prize or an award and does not meet the plain language requirements. Additionally, the AAO noted the online archive listed more than 100 winners and hundreds of finalists, authors self-nominate, pay a $69 entry fee per category, and have over 100 categories to choose from, indicating these are essentially vanity book awards. For the 'Winner' certificate, the petitioner failed to submit evidence demonstrating national or international recognition, including the number of other entries her book competed against.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) her membership card and Certificate of Membership for an association in the United States along with a 'Professional Code of Ethics' document; (2) evidence of membership in another organization with certificates stating she 'attained the level of Accredited Speaking Member' and received a specific designation; and (3) a July 2013 letter from Ms. [Manager, California Office] stating the petitioner holds a designation 'credited only to Professional Speakers, who have excelled in their keynotes, customer references, testimonials and earned more than $250,000 over a 2-year period,' which only 1% of professional speakers worldwide could substantiate.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that complying with a Code of Professional Ethics does not equate to 'outstanding achievements,' and the evidence did not show that members' achievements are judged by recognized national or international experts. For the Accredited Speaking Member designation, there was no documentary evidence such as bylaws, a constitution, or membership regulations showing outstanding achievements are required, as judged by recognized experts. Regarding Ms. [Manager]'s letter, she did not explain her affiliation with the organization or identify the source of her information about the designation's requirements. The AAO cited 1756, Inc. v. U.S. Att'y Gen. holding that USCIS need not credit conclusory assertions, and noted there was no documentary evidence from the organization itself showing the specific requirements.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a review of her book in a magazine (February 2004); (2) an article about her in another magazine; (3) an advertisement for her product in Equanimity (no date/author); (4) an advertisement for wedding dresses featuring photographs of her modeling a wedding gown in a magazine (article was about the designer, not her); (5) an article she authored in a publication (December 2002-January 2003); (6) an article about her entitled 'Mission to spread the happiness bug' (no title/date/author); (7) a June 8, 2007 interview in a UAE publication discussing corporate culture; (8) a July 7, 2007 article about an event (not about her); (9) an April 5, 2008 article about her in a magazine; (10) a partial August 8, 2000 article about male beauty treatments; (11) a March 25, 2010 article about scented candles; (12) an article with only legible title; and (13) on appeal, pointed to five books she authored claimed to be 'best-sellers' in Australia, UAE, UK, and Singapore.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found none of the submissions met the criterion. The petitioner failed to submit objective circulation figures to show the publications were major media. Advertisements are not the result of independent journalistic reportage. Material the petitioner authored herself does not qualify as 'published material about the alien'—that falls under the separate scholarly articles criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi). Articles that are not about the petitioner (such as articles about events or consumer products) do not qualify, citing Negro-Plumpe v. Okin. For items missing the title, date, or author, they failed the explicit requirements of the criterion. For the 'best-seller' claims, no supporting evidence (such as published rankings) was provided, and books constitute material she authored rather than published material about her. The AAO cited 1756, Inc. and Visinscaia holding USCIS need not rely on unsubstantiated claims.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The director discussed evidence submitted for this criterion, but the specific evidence is not detailed in the AAO decision. On appeal, the petitioner did not contest the director's findings or offer additional arguments.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that when an appellant fails to offer argument on an issue, that issue is abandoned, citing Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen. and Hristov v. Roark. Accordingly, the petitioner did not establish she meets this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters from peers and colleagues and asserted on appeal that she is 'the director of 8 companies in the media field, 5 of which are based here in the USA.' She pointed to her MBA, BSc in IT, training, Professional Speaking Experience, Media Training, and multiple companies established on 4 continents. She stated her doctorate was received based on her published papers on correlation topics, packaged in her unique way. She submitted EINs and Australian Company Numbers for her companies.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters from peers and colleagues failed to demonstrate her work was of major significance in the field, and the petitioner did not contest these findings or point to specific letters on appeal. The petitioner failed to provide tax returns showing her companies' annual revenues or business income. There was no documentary evidence showing her direction of eight companies rose to the level of original business-related contributions of major significance. The AAO noted the regulations include a separate criterion for leading/critical roles (h)(3)(viii), and these criteria are not interchangeable. Regarding academic degrees, the AAO cited Matter of New York State Dept. of Transportation holding that academic performance is not a specific prior achievement establishing ability to benefit the national interest. There was no evidence her published papers were frequently cited by business or mental health professionals or that her findings equated to original contributions of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted documentation showing she gave a Teachers' Mass Lecture, participated in a trade fair, participated in another event, and spoke at a Professional Speakers Association event.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that the petitioner's field is in business rather than the arts. The interpretation that 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vii) is limited to the visual arts is longstanding and has been upheld by a federal district court, citing Negro-Plumpe v. Okin (upholding the interpretation that performances by a performing artist do not fall under this criterion). As the petitioner is not a visual artist and has not created tangible pieces of art displayed at exhibitions or showcases, she did not meet the plain language requirements. The AAO noted that not every criterion will apply to every occupation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted she performed in a leading or critical role as 'Director of 8 successful media and charitable companies in USA & Australia as founding president and CEO.' She submitted a Company Profile organizational chart. She asserted she 'served on the board' of an Australian organization and submitted a 'Certificate of Recognition for stepping up to serve the membership' on a Marketing Team of a 2004 Committee, plus a Certificate of Recognition for 'valued assistance and guidance on Executive Committee' as Chapter 2004 Secretary. Both certificates were issued by the chapter President. She also submitted a letter from Ms. [Name] stating the chapter presented her with an Award in March 2012 and recognized her 'talents as an author, television host and philanthropist,' noting she plays an 'active role' in association activities.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Although the petitioner performed in a leading role as Director, President, and CEO, there was no documentary evidence showing which, if any, of her eight companies earned a distinguished reputation. The AAO cited Matter of Soffici regarding insufficient unsupported claims. Regarding the Australian organization, the certificates indicated her service was with the local chapter, not the board for the national association, and there was no evidence the chapter has a distinguished reputation. Regarding the other association, Ms. [Name] did not state that the petitioner's role as a member is leading or critical. The AAO explained a leading role is demonstrated by evidence of where the petitioner fits within the hierarchy, while a critical role is demonstrated by contributions to the organization. The petitioner did not provide an organizational chart showing her role within the hierarchy, failed to differentiate herself from other members, and did not establish the organization has a distinguished reputation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted she is 'in the top 1% of earners in the speaker industry earning $50,000 per keynote and $100,000 for consultancy and training for the day.' She submitted a July 2013 letter describing three tiers of speakers: Trainers and Experts ($1,000-$2,000/hour), Authors and Business Executives ($5,000-$10,000/hour), and Premium Speakers ($30,000-$100,000/hour including Sports Players and U.S. Presidents), stating they book the petitioner at $50,000 per hour and $100,000 for 6 hours consulting.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner failed to submit documentary evidence such as payroll records or income tax forms demonstrating actual remuneration earned. The AAO noted the letter came from a business entity controlled by the petitioner herself (as indicated in the Company Profile). The petitioner must demonstrate high salary 'in relation to others in the field,' not limited only to speakers booked by her own agency. The AAO cited Skokos v. U.S. Dept. of Homeland Security, Muni v. INS, Grimson v. INS, and Matter of Price for proper comparison methodology, requiring objective earnings data compared to those performing similar work during the same time period. The petitioner offered no reliable basis for comparison and no evidence of actual earnings for any specific year.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner asserted in her appellate submission that she has 'written 40 songs,' performed songs including specific titles, and written '3 movie screenplays.' She did not specifically claim eligibility for this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner did not submit documentation of record sales or box office receipts demonstrating commercial successes in the performing arts. The AAO cited Matter of Soffici that going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient. There was no documentary evidence of receipts or sales demonstrating commercial success in the recording or motion picture industries.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to satisfy the antecedent regulatory requirement of submitting qualifying evidence under at least three of the ten categories at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Under the Kazarian two-part approach, the AAO only counts the types of evidence at the initial stage, and since the petitioner did not meet at least three criteria, the proper conclusion is that she failed the regulatory requirement. The AAO noted that even if the requisite three categories had been met, the evidence was not indicative of a level of expertise consistent with the small percentage at the very top of the field or sustained national or international acclaim, but declined to explain that conclusion in a final merits determination. The petitioner did not establish eligibility under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, and the burden of proof was not met.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
1756, Inc. v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.D.C. 1990)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
Matter of New York State Department of Transportation, 22 I&N Dec. 215 (Comm'r 1998)
Skokos v. U.S. Dept. of Homeland Sec., 420 F. App'x 712 (9th Cir. 2011)
Muni v. INS, 891 F. Supp. 440 (N.D. Ill. 1995)
Grimson v. INS, 934 F. Supp. 965 (N.D. Ill. 1996)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner filed two Forms I-290B (Notice of Appeal or Motion) dated August 13, 2013 and August 14, 2013, appealing the director's July 17, 2013 decision; the AAO decision incorporates arguments from both filings. The AAO also noted that the petitioner abandoned the judging criterion issue by failing to contest the director's findings on appeal. Additionally, the AAO noted concerns about the vanity nature of certain book awards (self-nomination, entry fees, over 100 categories), and that a letter supporting the high salary claim came from a business entity controlled by the petitioner herself. No fraud findings or misrepresentation findings were made.
